speaker: interviewer
text: Gerda Kahan, tape number four. You said that your mother came three years later, so--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, my mother came three and a half years later. And two months later, I had my first daughter, which, to me, that was the biggest gift I could give her. And actually, I felt-- [BACKROUND NOISE] I was not quite 20-- that this child belongs to my mother. It gave her something, because she lost everything. And she never wanted to remarry. She said that-- she was beautiful, and she was young, and she didn't want to-- she said what she had, she can never replace. And just for the sake of being married, she'd rather devote her life to small children. And then when I had my daughter, that was her baby. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Who was-- who was Lillian named for?

speaker: interviewee
text: Lillian was named after her mother, my mother's mother. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And Lillian felt a special closeness to my mother, just as if she would be her mother, to a point that when my mother passed away, in 1975, Lillian did not open up a television, a radio, a movie for one year, the same way as I did. And I felt very close to my mother. It's not even normal how close I was to my mother. There was nothing that-- I was always with her. I was-- whatever I did was with her. I was talking to you about families. Just picture it, when you make a wedding. And we made beautiful weddings for our children. And you walk into a hall, which has about 350, 400 people. And there isn't one table that you can call those people belong to you or you belong to them.

speaker: interviewer
text: No relative tables.

speaker: interviewee
text: No relatives. It's a very painful feeling. It's-- but my children, it's not their fault. So they deserved it that they should have a normal life, to the best of my abilities. And I tried to do it.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what kind of life did they live in--

speaker: interviewee
text: My children?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: They were very good students. My-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] my middle daughter was a valedictorian. She skipped the last year of high school. She went to college. Oh, it's a-- it was not easy. But I went to the dean of admission at City College. And I explained to him that the schools don't want to give her her papers, only because they will lose the tuition. And he said, I understand. And I said, I did not have the possibilities to go-- to further my education. When I came here, I didn't speak the language. I couldn't afford it financially. But I am going to fight that my children have the best education that they possibly can get. And she did it. And she went to college after her junior year in high school. And the others were also very good students. And thanks God, they're happily married. And their children-- my grandson is also a valedictorian. My granddaughter just became Who's Who in college. And they have a very normal life, except that also their whole life was concentrated on only parents, never having grandparents, right, or extended families, and same as we concentrate all our love on them.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you were living in Washington Heights--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Washington Heights has a big survivor population.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right. And there were people that I knew from Berlin, even just by name. Some I didn't remember. But they were very, very nice. They-- they tried to compensate-- whether it was guilt, which I don't think so. But a lot of people felt that they survived-- you understand, here is that one child of a survivor. And they were friendly with my parents before the war, when they lived in Germany. They all lived in Berlin. So they were very nice to me. But it's not the same. You still are the outsider, right? And then--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever go to counseling or support groups or anything like that?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No, I couldn't. The same way as I cannot go to a diet center, which I would love to lose the weight. And I can't. I can't be told, go on a diet. Don't eat. I explained it to my doctor, Dr. Reinhardt. And I said, it's very hard that somebody tells me. I swore, I will never be hungry, that if I only survive, I will eat whatever I want. And it's very, very hard to tell me, don't eat. Go to Weight Watchers. Don't eat. Lose weight. I know it, that I should lose weight. And I would love to. But I cannot that somebody should tell it to me.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever get any reparations from the German government?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I did. Yes, I did, after a long battle. Because first of all, they said my parents were not registered in the telephone book in Berlin. My father never paid social security. After, I didn't give up. I took them five times to court in Berlin.

speaker: interviewee
text: All of a sudden, they [INAUDIBLE] I was a child. I started school in Berlin. So they knew they have to pay me for the education. I have been in concentration camps. I have been hurt there, mentally and physically, right? So they didn't want to pay all that. And especially the doctors in New York made it very, very difficult. They said I am lying. One talked to me for hours-- a psychiatrist-- for hours. And tell me how was it in camp, and tell me the whole story. And after he finished, so he says to me, so now tell me what was it in the concentration camp? I turned to him, and I said, you know what? If I would be telling you a lie, I would know exactly what you want to hear. If I tell you the truth, this is not pleasing to you. And I will not change my story, because it is the truth. So then they found us in the telephone book. And they found that my father paid social security in Berlin. And they had the address. All of a sudden, everything appeared, after I fought it. And so I get the [GERMAN]. And my husband-- unfortunately, I couldn't convince him that he should apply for the [GERMAN] because the word-- Which means?

speaker: interviewee
text: The word means, [GERMAN]. It-- you can't translate it exactly, but it means to make up for what we have done. And in his book, you cannot make up for it. It's-- as much as I would like him to get the money, I can't convince him, because he has certain principles.

speaker: interviewee
text: So now finally, I convinced him he should just take the money and give it away-- if they ever give it to him, if he lived that long. He applied for it now. So hopefully, we will be around to collect it. Did you ever go back to Germany?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I was invited by the Berlin magistrate three years ago. It was a tour for former people that lived in Berlin, or born in Berlin, to show them the city. And they were very, very nice. But it was extremely difficult for me. Because first of all, they were nice, and I couldn't accept that. Secondly, because I knew that they know I am Jewish. And a few years back, they wouldn't have been-- I was still the Jew, right? But they tried. And they said-- they apologized, that they are a new generation. And they don't want to be responsible for their forefathers. I wanted to tell them, I wasn't responsible for my forefathers, either. And I was punished. But one day they had a tour to Wannsee. Wannsee was a resort place outside of Berlin, where I have spent many summers as a child for vacation. And this is where all the-- Hitler had written, and Goebbels and Goering, all their laws against Jews and the destruction of Jews. And I have developed high temperature. And I got so sick, I couldn't go. I couldn't face it. And my husband, who was with me, begged me-- let's leave, after a day. Let's leave. I said, I'm here already. I want to see where I lived. So when we got there, I took a taxi, and we went to see the house where I was born, where I lived, which is [GERMAN]. And we took-- told the taxi driver to take us there.

speaker: interviewee
text: When we got there, he said, this is it. I said, I told you [GERMAN]. And he said, this is it! And there is nothing-- no house, nothing. And at first moment, it gave me a tremendous shock. I said, what do you mean, there is nothing? This is the house where I lived. This is the house where I grew up. And then I thought to myself, well, maybe this is right. The people that lived here are not here anymore, either. They were killed. Why should the house be standing? And went back into the taxi and quickly back to the hotel.

speaker: interviewee
text: But it takes out-- no matter how nice they are now, it's true they cannot make up. They cannot bring those people back to life. Those people had the same hopes as the German people have. They never committed a crime. And Hitler has taken out all the criminals from the jails and told them, have the Jews and do with them what you want. And they were ones that were watching us. They were the ones that were beating us. And they were the ones that were cursing us and called us names that were never invented. They invented those names. And you know, when you're young, and you are a child, and you come from a sheltered background, you cannot combine two things. You knew the Germans. You lived with them. You grew up there. I had beautiful times. And all of the sudden, those same Germans are hitting you and calling you names. Those are not the same people. They have been brainwashed. Or they have-- there is no change of heart with them. They are the same people that they were. But maybe they just cover it up better. I wish that they had changed.

speaker: interviewee
text: It's hard to trust them.

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know whether I trust them or don't trust them. You cannot make a whole people responsible for a group of people, that-- the same way as not every Jew is a criminal. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And you can only be a nation if you have everything. You have to have criminals. And you have to have police. Otherwise, you are not a nation.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me, do you get any satisfaction from the fact that Goeth, and Muller, and even-- that that these people were--

speaker: interviewee
text: Sentenced?

speaker: interviewer
text: Killed.

speaker: interviewee
text: Absolutely, absolutely.

speaker: interviewer
text: Eichmann in Israel.

speaker: interviewee
text: Goeth was hung in Krakow after the war. And Muller was sentenced. He was hiding. There are some people that were called to Germany to testify in court. I know them-- somebody from Belgium. But--

speaker: interviewer
text: Eichmann?

speaker: interviewee
text: How about the others? Yes. Oh, that was absolutely unbelievable, how he tortured people, how he destroyed human lives, how used the women for [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] exploring things to do. I just can't even think of it.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how about the fact that there's a state of Israel? Does that--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. That gives me a great satisfaction. I'm only sorry my father didn't live to see it, because he was a big Zionist. And in the beginning, when they had the debates about-- in the Security Council, at that time, at Lake Success, because the UN didn't exist yet-- we were there at every meeting, David and I. And we were there when the United States recognized the state of Israel. It was a jubilant moment in my life, and at the same time, great despair that my family didn't live to see that-- that we finally, whether it's the blood of the 6 million that created it, whether it's the conscience of the world that created the state of Israel, it doesn't matter. I'm upset what's going on now, but show me one country in the world that is winning a war that you didn't start and is giving back territories.

speaker: interviewee
text: There is no such as. Russia didn't give back. Germany didn't give back. There is no country in the world that just is told by others, give back so you will have peace. What kind of a peace is that? You're negotiating with whom? With a criminal. We are coming to square one. We're starting all over again. How about your children? How do they how do they reconcile things with-- how do they live with the memories that you have, actually?

speaker: interviewee
text: See what it is-- they can't have the same memories, because they didn't know the people, right? They don't have the same link with the past as I have. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] They know about it. They hear it. But they can't feel it. You understand? When I tell them something about my brother, they know how I adored him. And everybody tells them what a special human being he was. They can relate to it, but they cannot feel it. There is a difference.

speaker: interviewee
text: Because I grew up in the same household with him, right? I knew that every night, when I called, my father adored me. I was the only granddaughter in the family, whether it was skiing, skating, swimming, gym, you name it, music, the theater. I performed in the theater. You name it. I had it all, right? In the evening, when I went to bed already, I used to call my father-- daddy, water. He never said no to me. How can they understand that? They can't feel-- they know I have lost my father. My father was killed. My brother was killed. My aunts, my uncles, my cousins, my grandparents. They know it, right?

speaker: interviewee
text: But they cannot understand those moments, those precious holiday feelings. I mean, I hated Pesach for many years, because it brought back such severe pain, because the memories were there. And I couldn't-- I would have liked to put them at the table, and they are not there. It's only in my memory. I didn't like any holiday to speak of, because I was missing that one link that connected the holiday to my family. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I'm sure, in their own way, they feel for it. But it-- you can't expect it. The whole world didn't understand it. And even so, they knew it.

speaker: interviewee
text: Because when you read today that they knew it, Roosevelt knew it, this one, everybody knew. And they didn't want to do anything about it. It was only Jews. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] You're talking here about second-class citizens, right? By whom? By whose standards? In conclusion, is there some kind of a message that you'd like to give to your children and to anybody who watches this tape?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I would very much like them that they should never forget it. Never. That it's true that it happened, that if they see whatever it is-- if you see an injustice done to any nation, to any people, to any color, to any creed, to any religion, stand up and speak up. Don't let that repeat itself. Because it's not only a punishment to the people you do it to, you destroy the world.

speaker: interviewee
text: Because actually, it destroyed the world. Because we won the war, right? We came out. But there was never peace. Since 1933, there was no peace in the world. The World War I was won. Vietnam was won. Korea was won. The World War II was won. Everything was won. And there is no peace. So eventually, we will destroy the world, if we will continue being against each other.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I am only saying that, not so much to the world. I'm saying that to the Jews, that one Jew has to stand up for each other. Because if we don't like each other, if we have no feelings for the ones that have been punished and suffered, nobody else will. And I hold it very much against the Jewish organizations during the war that were silent, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] that they could have done a lot. There was a time when the American government was requested the Jewish organizations $150 per person, that they would let-- they should pay to the German government. They needed trucks and cars. And there was silence. Nobody heard about it. All the Hungarian Jews could have been saved. They have been at home till 1944. By that time, I didn't know any more what month of the year it is, never mind holidays, or the day of the week-- nothing, nothing.

speaker: interviewee
text: And why? For money. And because it were Jews. But even the Jews themselves didn't want to know. They were afraid to rock the boat. They were afraid that the government, the American government, will look poorly at them. They should have shouted in the streets that our brothers are being killed. And there is nobody to stand up for-- like the Germans kept telling us, where is your God? Where is your God? Because how many times you heard them scream, [HEBREW], right? The Jews screaming? Right! And the Germans said, where is your God? And your relative, Rosenfeld-- they called Roosevelt-- where is he? Why doesn't he do anything for you? They, themselves, couldn't understand it. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] If we ever had from the Wehrmacht-- that's the army, the German army-- if they supervised us or whatever, they were much more normal. They were not so destroyed like the SS or the Gestapo, right? So they talked to us like to a human being. And they couldn't understand that the world is silent. They said, what's going on with the world? They were doubting it. So can you picture it, how we felt, that we are alone, that no matter what we do, there is nobody to listen to us. How many nights I was lying there on that barrack there, on that double-decker, and I was thinking to myself, my poor relatives that are outside of this hell-- what they are going through. Little did I know, it doesn't bother them. They had a problem, because they only could get one chicken, right, or only five pounds of sugar. Rationing during the war.

speaker: interviewee
text: I was so sorry for them, not realizing that I am the one to feel sorry for myself. Nobody else did. I remember, I came here-- and I came from a big city, right? I came from Berlin. I walked on West End Avenue to visit somebody. And I walked into the building. Naturally, the woman saw right away that I am European. I'm a refugee.

speaker: interviewee
text: And she says, you see this button? You push this button and you will fly up-- the elevator. I thought to myself, is she crazy or am I crazy? She didn't-- she didn't realize that you came from a very cultured--

speaker: interviewee
text: It doesn't matter. But they were not there when I needed them. Don't tell me now what an elevator is. Or, you're going to have something to eat you never had in your life, ice cream. You understand, it was-- but I don't know whether the people were so stupid or they didn't want to understand. They didn't want to understand that the Jews of Europe had feelings. They had skin. They had blood in their veins. They had a heart. And they had brains. And they were functioning, one better than the other, but everybody somehow lived. And the whole world turned against them and closed the door. Never mind closed the doors so they couldn't give us visas, but they didn't even speak up! Never mind you don't want us. You shipped boats back to Germany. Fine. You don't want us. But speak up. At least yell. Let it be heard. Nothing. It's very sad. And I only hope that it will never repeat itself, that all the sufferings that I have done-- and believe me, I didn't tell you half of it, because I-- you cannot put it into words. It's impossible that I have covered for my great, great, grandchildren, mine and the whole world. Because it's unbelievable.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you freeze to death, and you have nothing to cover yourself, and you're hungry that you're starving, and you only hear shooting around you and killing, what is there to look forward? Many people, many said, what's the use? They kill us. They kill us. And I wouldn't want to give them the satisfaction. And maybe that's what helped me to survive, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] right?

speaker: interviewee
text: [SIGHS] And you come back. And you start putting together, piece by piece. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] It's not easy, by far not easy. Even when I knew that I am going to speak to you, it took me a few nights to, like, assemble my mind.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is it possible that you can speak about it? Is it possible that you will make sense? Is it possible that you do justice to those that cannot speak? Can you portray the real picture of what people have suffered? How they were bitten by dogs, every time they were coming into work or going out, and when they pulled them by their pants, and when they bit into their legs. And there was no medication. And and the disgrace and the humility. Can you give them justice by speaking about them? Do you have the power in you to portray it that the world should be able to understand what happened, that nobody should ever be able to say, it did not happen? [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] This is-- my biggest problem today is to face it that I didn't harm anybody in the past that didn't make it. You've you've spoken beautifully. And we really thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: For what? I thank you.
